title: Abstract
content: Visible-light-driven Cr-doped SrTiO 3 was synthesized through ultrasonic chemical method.Its specific surface area is 54.3 m 2 g -1 due to the relatively small particle size.X-ray diffraction pattern and X-ray photoelectron spectra confirmed the crystal structure and Sr substitution.UV-vis absorption spectra indicated that the Cr doping can lead to visible-light absorption.In addition, the oxygen vacancy in Crdoped SrTiO 3 also showed visible-light absorption.The obtained Cr-doped SrTiO 3 was used to photocatalytically reduce CO 2 into CH 4 with a yield of 8.8 mmol g -1 after 10-h reaction.Moreover, the Crdoped SrTiO 3 exhibited good photostability.

title: Experimental
content: All of the chemicals were bought from Sinopharm Chemicals (analytical grade) and used as received without any further purification.The SrTiO 3 (denoted as STO) was synthesized through sonochemistry at room temperature.The titanium source used in the production of SrTiO 3 is Ti(OH) 4 .In a typical procedure, about 5 mmol of Ti(SO 4 ) 2 and 20 mmol of KOH were dissolved in water, respectively.Then the two solutions were mixed together, followed by centrifugation.The obtained white precipitate was washed with water for three times so as to remove the adsorbed SO 4 2-and K ions.The above white precipitate together with 100 mmol of KOH and 5 mmol of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 were added into 50 mL of water, respectively.Then the obtained suspension was added into the Sr(NO 3 ) 2 solution, followed by ultrasonication for 2 h.After being centrifuged and washed copiously with water, the white SrTiO 3 powder was acquired. The Cr-doped SrTiO 3 (CSTO) was synthesized with a similar procedure to STO, but 0.25 mmol of Cr(NO 3 ) 3 and 4.75 mmol of Sr (NO 3 ) 2 were used instead of just 5 mmol of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 for the synthesis of STO. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on a Bruker D8 diffractometer (Cu Ka, l 0.15418 nm) to study the crystal structure.The scanning was performed at a speed of 0.1 s with a diffraction angle ranging from 20 to 80 .The morphology, dispersion and size distribution were monitored by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS).Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were carried out with 200 kV (Tecnai G2 20 s-TWIN).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to determine the composition and the valence band maximum of the as-prepared catalysts.UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were collected on a Lambda 750 UVvisibleNIR spectrophotometer. In a typical catalytic experiment, 20 mg of catalyst suspended in 80 mL of H 2 O was used and the photocatalytic system was as reported previously 22.The system was sealed in order to prevent any leakage and maintain a positive pressure (25 kPa) of high-purity CO 2 (99.999).The CO 2 was kept flowing through the reaction system for 30 min by bubbling into the water, and eventually into the inlet of a gas chromatography (GC).The system was free of air during this procedure.The GC inlet was kept clean, i.e., no contaminants of previous samples or air.Moreover, CO 2 was saturated in the water and adsorbed on the catalyst surface to achieve equilibrium between adsorption and desorption.The system was irradiated by visible light (l 420 nm) from a xenon lamp (300 W).The reaction was kept for 10 h and the product was analyzed every one hour by a GC (Agilent 7890) with one thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and two flame ionization detectors (FID), using helium as carrier gas.To confirm that the product was due to the photoreduction (i.e., CO 2 was the only carbon source and the catalyst was involved in the photochemical reaction), three control experiments were carried out under exactly the same conditions except (1) under dark condition, (2) without catalysts, and (3) with N 2 instead of CO 2 .No any products were detected in these control experiments, suggesting that the observed product was from the photoreduction of CO 2 owing to the catalytic effect.In addition, the same catalyst have been undergone the reactions for at least three times so as to study the stability of the obtained catalyst.

title: Results and discussion
content: The SEM images of STO and CSTO are shown in Fig. 1.Both the STO and CSTO are nearly cubic nanoparticles assembled by small nanoplates.The former is about 300 nm in size and the latter is a little smaller.The EDS results confirm that the STO sample only contains the elements of Sr, Ti, and O; while besides these three elements the CSTO sample also has Cr (Fig. 1).The measured Fig. 3. XRD patterns of CSTO before and after photocatalytic reaction, and asprepared STO.Inset is the zoom-in of (11 0) peak for the as-prepared samples.atomic ratio of CrSr from EDS is about 229 for CSTO, which is slightly larger than that of the precursor (119).This may be because the modified Cr is prone to locate adjacent to the crystal surface.Moreover, the Cr element distributes homogeneously in the CSTO sample. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is used to analyze the chemical composition since it can afford the information about oxidation states of the elements in the sample and the difference in binding energy (BE) of the same element in different samples.The peaks at 132.87 and 134.59 eV (Fig. 2a) are ascribed to Sr3d 52 and Sr3d 32 , respectively.The BE of 458.14 and 463.88 eV (Fig. 2b) is the characteristic signal of the Ti2p 32 and Ti2p 12 , respectively.All these peaks of Sr3d and Ti2p are typical BE values for the perovskite SrTiO 3 .As for the CSTO, however, it is noted that the peak positions corresponding to Sr3d increase by 0.27 eV compared to STO and 0.07 eV for Ti2p (Fig. 2).This supports that the doped Cr 3 has a larger influence on the Sr sites than that on the Ti sites, which is considered the evidence of Sr-site substitution 14.Thus, Cr is successfully incorporated into the STO.The respective peak of Cr2p 32 and Cr2p 12 is at 577.09 and 586.53 eV (Fig. 2e), which is assigned to Cr 3 .No signal that can be attributed to Cr 6 can be observed in the XPS spectrum. The purity and crystallinity of a sample can be detected by XRD.The obtained samples are with cubic structure according to the XRD patterns (Fig. 3), suggesting the formation of SrTiO 3 (JCPDS 74-1296).The peak of (0 0 1) has a larger 2u value and is sharper for STO than that for CSTO, indicating that the crystallite size becomes smaller after doping and the crystallinity decreases as well.It is important to know the doping sites of Cr (Sr-substituted or Tisubstituted) since the doped SrTiO 3 can show different photocatalytic activity based on the results of theoretical calculations and experiments 32, which can be investigated by the shift of the diffraction peaks.If Cr substitutes Sr, the interplaner spacing would decrease, while it would increase if Cr locates in the Ti site because the ionic radius of these three ions increases in the order of Ti 4 (0.0605) Cr 3 (0.0615 nm) Sr 2 (0.118 nm).In terms of the (11 0) plane, the peak position shifts to a higher 2u value upon Cr doping, i.e., the interplaner spacing becomes smaller.Thus, the obtained CSTO is Sr-substituted Cr-doped SrTiO 3 , which agrees with the above conclusions drawn from XPS results. The HRTEM images and selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED) are used to further study the crystalline details as they can provide information about the crystal structure and the changes in lattice spacing after Cr doped.According to the HRTEM image (Fig. 4a), the lattice spacing of STO is 0.28 nm, which is consistent with the (11 0) lattice spacing of the perovskite phase SrTiO 3 with cubic structure 33.The (11 0) lattice spacing of CSTO becomes 0.27 nm (Fig. 4b), which is smaller than that of the pure phase.Hence, Cr is doped into the lattice by substitution of Sr, which agrees with the above conclusions.In addition, both the STO and CSTO samples are well crystallized according to the SAED patterns (Fig. 4). Fig. 5 shows the UV-visible absorption spectra of STO and CSTO.The absorption edge of band gap for both STO and CSTO locates in the UV region.The respective band gap of STO and CSTO can be determined to be 3.45 and 3.35 eV from the inset of Fig. 5.So the doping of Cr slightly narrows the band gap, which is consistent with the report that the band gap becomes narrower after Cr doped into STO by elevating the VB 34.Moreover, a sub-band gap can be identified to be 2.4 eV in the CSTO sample.All these phenomena are ascribed to the doping of Cr into the crystal lattice of STO.The CB of STO is mainly comprised of Ti3d orbital, which is approximately 2.2 eV higher than that of the Cr3d that is 1 eV higher than the top of the VB of STO (mainly O2p orbital) 31.In addition, a broad sub-peak located at around 610 nm can be observed in the spectrum of CSTO, implying the formation of new states, which can be ascribed to the oxygen vacancy 35363738.It has been reported that the Cr plays the role of a seed for the formation of oxygen vacancy 37.Thus, the STO sample exhibits visible-light response after Cr doping. The VB of STO and CSTO is determined respectively to be 3.14 and 2.20 eV in terms of the XPS valence-band spectra (Fig. 6).Provided the 4.5 eV with respect to the vacuum level is 0 V against standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) and the work function of the XPS instrument is 4.62 eV, the VB of STO and CSTO is calculated to be 3.26 and 2.32 V (vs SHE), respectively.So the respective CB of STO and CSTO is calculated to be A0.19 and A1.03 V (vs SHE) according to the potential of VB and band gap.Therefore, the alignment of energy levels can be illustrated as shown in Fig. 7.The visible-light response in CSTO corresponds to the absorption from the VB to the defect levels andor that from the defect levels to the CB of CSTO. The reaction product is CH 4 as analyzed by GC and no other gas or liquid products can be detected.In the three control experiments, no any products can be probed.As shown in Table 1, no any organic molecules can be detected for the blank experiments using N 2 instead of CO 2 , indicating no original carbohydrate or other carbon residue present in the reaction system.As no any products can be observed without irradiation, in addition, the reaction is triggered by the photons.Furthermore, no CH 4 is observed without the utilization of catalyst, suggesting that the catalyst serves as a bridge between the CO 2 transformation and photons.Since STO does not respond to the visible light, no product can be detected over STO upon visible-light illumination (Fig. 8a), while the yield of CH 4 is 8.8 mmol g -1 over CSTO after 10-h reaction. The stability of the CSTO has been investigated by repeating the reduction reaction for at least three times.It is found that the yield of CH 4 after 10 h decreases from 8.8 to 8.1 mmol g -1 after three runs (Fig. 8b).Moreover, no obvious change can be observed in the XRD patterns of the CSTO before and after the reaction, as shown in Fig. 3.The slight decrease observed in the production yield of CH 4 after each run may be ascribed to the minor reduction in the active sites of the photocatalyst (such as the oxygen vacancy) upon photocatalysis.Thus, the Cr-doped SrTiO 3 catalyst exhibits good photostability and steady visible-light photocatalytic activity over the conversion of CO 2 into CH 4 . The mechanism of photoreduction of CO 2 with CSTO can be elucidated using Fig. 7.The CSTO exhibits visible-light response, as shown in Fig. 5. Once upon the light absorption, the exited electrons transfer to the CB of CSTO.Since the potential of CB (A1.03 V) is much more negative than the redox potential of CH 4 CO 2 , the CO 2 can be reduced by the CB electrons into CH 4 .Meanwhile, the holes left in the VB of CSTO can oxidize H 2 O.In addition, the oxygen vacancies present in the CSTO can act as the active sites for the chemical adsorption of CO 2 , leading to the production of carbonate ions 18 and, thereby, facilitating the photocatalytic reduction.Moreover, the CSTO has a relatively large BET specific surface area (54.3 m 2 g -1 ), which is also in favor of the photocatalysis and is derived from the nitrogen adsorptiondesorption curve with PP 0 -range of 0-1 (Fig. 9).This value is slightly larger than the one for STO (30.7 m 2 g -1 ), as the doping of Cr leads to a smaller particle size (Fig. 1).

title: Conclusions
content: A facile ultrasonic chemical approach can ensure the doping of Cr 3 at Sr site in the SrTiO 3 , leading to visible-light response and endowing the CB electrons with a potential negative enough and, thereby, resulting in the photoreduction of CO 2 .The oxygen vacancy and comparatively large specific surface area are also in favor of the production yield of CH 4 .Moreover, the obtained photocatalyst exhibits good photostability.Thus, the Cr doped SrTiO 3 is a promising catalyst for the visible-light photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 into value-added chemicals. Fig.1.SEM images and corresponding EDS mapping of the as-prepared STO and CSTO.Scale bar for the inset SEM images is 500 nm. Fig. 2. XPS spectra of (a) Sr and (b) Ti in STO; (c) Sr, (d) Ti and (e) Cr in CSTO. Fig. 4. HRTEM images of (a) STO and (b) CSTO.The corresponding SAED pattens are shown in (c) STO and (d) CSTO. Fig. 5. UV-vis absorption spectra of STO and CSTO. Fig. 6.XPS valance band spectra of STO and CSTO. Fig. 7. Schematic illustration of band-energy alignment of STO and doped CSTO (not to scale).The redox potential of CH 4 CO 2 is also presented here. Fig. 8. (a) CH 4 production yield as a function of reaction time over STO and CSTO, (b) repeated run of photocatalytic CH 4 evolution. Photocatalytic results of different experiments.
